policy_statement: Improve transit reliability by bringing infrastructure into a state of good repair and fund State of Good Repair with at least $300 million annually (TLU.1-2).
mechanism_description: Bring transit infrastructure into a state of good repair and fund it to improve transit reliability, supporting transit use and reducing transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transit system performance investment → ↑ transit reliability/attractiveness → mode shift (↓ VMT) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public agencies invest in maintaining/rehabilitating transit assets (tracks, signals, vehicles, stations, power, etc.) to improve on-time performance and reduce breakdowns/slow zones.  
(2) Mediating physical/process link: Improved service quality increases transit capacity and user utility (reliability, travel time consistency), inducing riders to choose transit more often and/or enabling service frequency improvements; this reduces vehicle miles traveled (VMT) by private cars and associated fuel use.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and other GHGs) from reduced car travel; potentially additional reductions if better-performing transit allows more efficient operations (e.g., fewer detours/idle time), though the dominant effect is mode shift/VMT reduction.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions due to increased transit reliability leading to mode shift from private vehicle travel to transit.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: SOGR investment → higher reliability → higher ridership/mode shift → lower VMT and fuel combustion → lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While SOGR can also improve resilience (fewer weather-related failures) and resource efficiency (maintaining assets), the largest climate-relevant impact across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction from avoided car travel.  
3) Edge cases applied: If SOGR is justified mainly for climate-proofing or extreme-weather hardening with little expectation of mode shift, it could lean adaptation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly links SOGR to transit use and emissions reduction, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy framed as improving transit reliability via SOGR to support transit use has the same principal emissions-reduction pathway through mode shift/avoided VMT.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: Any further subclassification (e.g., “mode shift,” “operations efficiency,” “resilience co-benefit”) requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “state of good repair” program is implemented primarily as climate adaptation/hardening (e.g., floodproofing tunnels) without a material ridership/mode-shift effect, the dominant pathway could shift toward Adaptation; otherwise Mitigation is appropriate.
key_indicators: “Improve transit reliability”; “bringing infrastructure into a state of good repair”; “Adequately fund State of Good Repair”; “at least $300 million annually”
co_benefits: Improved safety, reduced service disruptions, accessibility/mobility benefits, potential air-quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a target-setting element due to the “$300 million annually” minimum, but the operative mechanism is a funding/investment commitment for asset repair rather than a performance or emissions target.
